worth_it_explanation: Content classification is the signal buyers read to decide whether their ads belong next to a page, so an inaccurate or missing category can either repel demand that would have bid or attract demand that lands an ad on content the advertiser meant to avoid. The check is a review of the content category and sensitive-content signals a publisher already passes through Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete classification the publisher can correct or add.
intro: Content classification is how a publisher tells buyers what a page or app is about, using the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy categories carried in the bid request. Microsoft Monetize passes those category signals and any sensitive-content flags to demand so that buyers can target context they want and apply the brand-safety rules they set. This subcategory covers whether those signals are accurate and complete, both the topic categories that describe the content and the sensitive-content flags that mark material a buyer may want to avoid. These checks confirm that the classification a publisher sends matches what is actually on the page, so demand bids on the inventory it intends to and steers clear of the inventory it does not.

audit_checks.title: Content categories are missing or do not match the page
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Content categories are the contextual signal buyers read to decide whether to bid and what their ads sit next to. When the category is missing, demand that targets context cannot find the inventory and bids thin out. When the category is wrong, an ad can land on content the advertiser meant to avoid, which erodes the buyer's trust in the supply and can pull spend away over time.

educate.base:
A content category is the label a publisher attaches to a page or app screen to describe what it is about, and Microsoft Monetize passes that label to buyers inside the bid request. The shared vocabulary for these labels is the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy, a numbered list of topics that every party in the supply chain reads the same way. When a sports recap carries the category for sports and a recipe page carries the category for food and drink, a buyer targeting either context can recognize the inventory and bid on it.

Two failures break that signal. The first is a missing category, where a page reaches the auction with no classification at all. Buyers who target context have nothing to match against, so demand that would have bid on the page never sees it, and the impression clears at a lower price or goes unsold. The second is a wrong category, where the page carries a label that does not match the content, for example a politics article tagged as entertainment. A wrong category is worse than a missing one because it actively misroutes demand. A buyer targeting the labeled context wins an impression that is not what they paid for, and an advertiser who excluded the real topic finds their ad there anyway.

Classification can drift in a few predictable places. A site section can be mapped to a category once and then repurposed for different content without anyone updating the mapping. A content management system can apply a default category to every page in a template rather than the topic of the specific page. A migration to the IAB Content Taxonomy 3.x numbering can leave some pages on the older 1.x or 2.x codes, which some buyers no longer read.

A few signs point to category signals that need a look:
  • Pages or sections reach the auction with no content category attached
  • A single default category is applied across a template rather than the topic of each page
  • Section-to-category mappings have not been reviewed since the content in those sections changed
  • Categories still use a deprecated taxonomy version that some demand partners no longer recognize
You may want to confirm that the categories a publisher sends match the content on the page and use a taxonomy version buyers still read. One approach is to sample pages across each major section, compare the category in the bid request against what the page is actually about, and correct the mappings that have drifted.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, sections can carry distinct topics that each deserve their own category, and a breaking-news page can sit in a section whose default category no longer fits. You may want to sample a page from each section, confirm its category matches the content rather than a section-wide default, and pay particular attention to sections that mix topics such as a general news feed.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages describe specific goods, so a generic shopping label may be too coarse for buyers who target a narrower context such as a product type. You may want to check that product and category pages carry a category specific enough for contextual demand to recognize, rather than one broad commerce label across the whole catalog.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid.js First Party Data for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review the content categories attached to a sample of pages across each major section, and compare each one against what the page is actually about and against the taxonomy version demand partners expect.

page_inspection.label: Page and bid-request inspection
steps: Pick a sample of pages spanning each major section or content type on the site or app.

Open the browser developer tools network panel on each sampled page and inspect the outgoing bid request for the content category fields, for example the OpenRTB 'cat', 'sectioncat', and 'pagecat' values.
Record the category attached to each sampled page and whether it matches the topic the page actually covers.
Note the taxonomy version in use, for example whether categories are IAB Content Taxonomy 1.x, 2.x, or 3.x codes, and whether any sampled page carries no category at all.

monetize_console.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the inventory or placement configuration in the Microsoft Monetize console and review how content categories are assigned to sites, sections, or placements.
Record whether categories are set per section or content type, or whether a single default category is applied broadly across a template.
Compare the configured categories against the sample of pages inspected in the network panel to confirm the console settings match what reaches the auction.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each sampled page, the content category sent in the bid request, the taxonomy version, and whether the category matches the page topic. A setup where sampled pages carry accurate categories in a taxonomy version demand partners read passes. A setup where pages reach the auction with no category, a default category that ignores the page topic, or a deprecated taxonomy version is a fail recorded with the pages or sections affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the content category, taxonomy version, and page topic for each sampled page above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} sends content categories that are missing or do not match the page on part of the sampled inventory. Content categories are the contextual signal buyers in Microsoft Monetize read to decide whether to bid and what their ads sit next to, so a missing category thins out demand that targets context and a wrong category misroutes ads onto content the advertiser meant to avoid. {{voice_recommend}} sampling pages across each major section, comparing the category in the bid request against the topic the page actually covers, and correcting the mappings that have drifted using a current Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy version that demand partners still read. After the change, recheck a fresh sample to confirm the categories match the content.

audit_checks.title: Sensitive content is not flagged for buyer brand-safety controls
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Sensitive-content flags are how buyers apply the brand-safety rules they set, so content that should carry a flag but does not can clear an advertiser's exclusions and place an ad next to material the brand meant to avoid. A pattern of unflagged sensitive content erodes buyer trust in the supply, and buyers who detect it through their own verification can throttle or stop bidding on the publisher's inventory.

educate.base:
Beyond saying what a page is about, content classification also marks content a buyer may want to avoid. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy carries a set of sensitive-content categories, sometimes called the brand-safety categories, that cover topics such as crime, hate speech, adult content, and graphic violence. When a publisher flags a page that falls into one of these topics, a buyer's brand-safety rules can read the flag and hold their ad off the page. When the flag is absent, the buyer's exclusion has nothing to act on.

The risk sits with content that should carry a sensitive flag and does not. A news section that covers crime, a forum that hosts user-generated posts, or an editorial page on a contested topic can all reach the auction looking like ordinary inventory. An advertiser who excluded that topic wins the impression anyway because the signal that would have triggered the exclusion was never sent. The ad runs next to content the brand meant to steer clear of, and the publisher carries the consequence in the form of complaints, chargebacks, or a buyer who pulls spend.

A second risk is over-flagging, where ordinary content is marked sensitive by an overly broad rule. Over-flagging is the safer error of the two because it costs revenue rather than trust. Buyers honoring the flag will simply not bid, so the impression clears low or goes unsold. The audit looks at both directions, but the under-flagging case carries the higher stakes because it is the one a buyer discovers after the fact.

A few signs point to sensitive-content flagging that needs review:
  • Sections that cover sensitive topics such as crime or graphic news carry no sensitive-content classification
  • User-generated content runs with no flag despite holding posts the publisher does not pre-screen
  • The publisher relies entirely on a default classifier with no review of how it handles edge-case content
  • Buyer complaints or verification reports point to ads appearing next to content that should have been flagged
You may want to confirm that content falling into the sensitive categories is flagged so buyer brand-safety rules can act on it. One approach is to map each section against the IAB sensitive-content categories, sample the sections most likely to hold flaggable material, and confirm the flag reaches the auction.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, hard-news sections covering crime, conflict, or disaster routinely hold content that falls into the sensitive categories, and a comments section adds user-generated material the publisher does not pre-screen. You may want to confirm those sections carry the appropriate sensitive-content classification rather than the same neutral category as soft features, so a buyer's exclusion can act on them.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, most catalog pages are unlikely to be sensitive, but user reviews, community forums, and editorial pieces on regulated products such as alcohol or supplements can fall into flaggable territory. You may want to check that those specific surfaces carry a sensitive-content flag where the topic warrants it, rather than assuming the whole commerce site is uniformly safe.

learn_more.source_title: First Party Data - Prebid Server for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review which sections and content types could hold sensitive material, and confirm that those surfaces carry the appropriate sensitive-content classification in the signals sent to buyers.

section_mapping.label: Section and content-type mapping
steps: List the site or app sections and content types, and mark which ones could hold content in the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) sensitive-content categories, for example crime, adult content, hate speech, or graphic violence.
Pay particular attention to surfaces with user-generated content, such as comments, forums, or reviews, where the publisher does not pre-screen what appears.
Record which of those sections currently carry a sensitive-content classification and which do not.

monetize_console.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the inventory configuration in the Microsoft Monetize console and review the content classification and any sensitive-content or content-safety settings applied to sites, sections, or placements.
Record whether the sensitive-content flags configured in the console match the sections the mapping identified as potentially sensitive.
Note any section flagged as sensitive by the mapping that carries no corresponding flag in the console, and any ordinary section that appears over-flagged.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each section or content type that could hold sensitive material, whether a sensitive-content flag is sent and whether the flag matches the content. A setup where sections holding sensitive content carry the appropriate flag, and ordinary content is not over-flagged, passes. A setup where sections that hold crime, adult, hateful, or graphic content reach the auction with no sensitive-content flag is a fail recorded with the sections affected, and over-flagging is recorded as a lower-stakes secondary finding.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each potentially sensitive section and whether it carries a matching sensitive-content flag above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not flag some content that falls into the sensitive categories, so buyer brand-safety rules have no signal to act on. Sensitive-content flags are how buyers in Microsoft Monetize apply the exclusions they set, so an unflagged crime, adult, or graphic page can clear an advertiser's exclusion and place an ad next to material the brand meant to avoid, which erodes trust in the supply. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each section against the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) sensitive-content categories, paying particular attention to user-generated surfaces, and confirming that sections holding sensitive material carry the appropriate flag in the signals sent to demand. After the change, recheck the flagged sections and confirm ordinary content is not over-flagged in a way that costs fill.
